Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic. I didn't have a set schedule because I was dealing with a lot of stuff outside of school, so I'd just study whenever I could grab a few hours. Most of my studying happened late at night when things were quieter, and I'd listen to lectures or audio materials since that's how I learn best. I averaged maybe 12 hours a week, but those hours were scattered all over the place depending on what crisis was happening that week.
Q2: My pattern varied wildly depending on deadlines and whatever else was going on in my life. Some weeks I'd barely touch anything, then I'd have these intense bursts where I'd cram everything in before a deadline. I couldn't maintain regular study times even if I wanted to because my situation at home and with money kept changing. Deadlines were basically my only real structure.
Q3: Usually a deadline would prompt me to log in, or sometimes I'd get a notification about something due soon. I'd also log in when I felt that anxious pressure building up, like I knew I was falling behind and needed to catch up. Sometimes boredom actually pushed me to check in, just to see what I was missing. But it was rarely just casual browsing—there was always some urgency behind it.
Q4: It became routine during the really intense weeks when assignments were piling up. I'd log in first thing in the morning and last thing at night just to make sure I wasn't missing anything critical. The anxiety of potentially failing made it habitual for a while. But once the pressure eased, that routine would slip away again until the next crisis hit.
Q5: The video lectures were honestly a lifesaver for me since I'm an auditory learner. I watched over 200 minutes of them because I could actually absorb information that way better than reading. I'd often play them while doing other things or replay sections I didn't catch the first time. The task completion checklists were also helpful because they gave me a clear path of what needed to get done.
Q6: I pretty much ignored the discussion forums—I only posted twice the whole course. It felt like a waste of time when I was already stretched thin, and I didn't see how chatting with classmates would help my grade directly. Reading materials I'd usually skim or skip entirely unless they were absolutely necessary for an assignment. When you're low on resources and time, you have to prioritize ruthlessly.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to an assignment or exam. If I could see how it would help me complete something that was graded, I'd do it. The mindfulness intervention they had us do actually helped with my anxiety, so that felt valuable in a different way. But anything that seemed like busywork or optional enrichment got pushed to the bottom of my list.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't afford. When you're barely keeping your head above water, you focus on what's mandatory and let everything else go. Required activities had clear consequences for not doing them, so they got my attention. Optional stuff just made me feel guilty for not having time to engage with it, which added to my stress honestly.
Q9: Absolutely, I did a ton of studying that never showed up in the LMS. I'd download lecture audio and listen to it on my phone while commuting or working. I also used free resources I found online—YouTube videos, podcasts about the subject matter. Since I don't have a lot of money for textbooks, I got creative with what I could access for free outside the system.
Q10: There were many times I was genuinely studying but the LMS wouldn't know. I'd take handwritten notes while listening to downloaded lectures, then review those notes repeatedly without ever logging back in. Sometimes I'd study with the few peers I connected with in person, quizzing each other verbally. My best learning happened through listening and talking, not clicking around on a platform.
Q11: Yeah, this was my main strategy actually. I'd access a video or document once, download it or take detailed notes, and then never go back to it on the LMS. Why would I keep logging in to rewatch something when I could just have it on my phone? It saved data and time, and I could study anywhere without needing internet access, which was important given my situation.
Q12: There were definitely periods when everything felt impossible. When money got really tight or family stuff escalated, I just couldn't focus on coursework. My attendance dropped to like 33% because sometimes I literally couldn't make it to class. The anxiety would spike and I'd feel paralyzed, even though I knew I needed to engage. Those were the hardest moments.
Q13: All of those things affected me constantly. Fatigue was huge because I wasn't sleeping well due to anxiety. Confusion about course material made me avoid logging in because I didn't want to face how lost I felt. Competing responsibilities—work, family obligations—meant the LMS was always fighting for my attention against more immediate survival needs. I had to triage constantly.
Q14: Oh definitely. Sometimes I'd log in and just stare at the screen, scrolling through materials without anything actually registering. My mind would be elsewhere, worrying about bills or other problems. I'd watch a video and realize at the end that I hadn't absorbed a single thing. Those sessions felt like going through the motions to trick myself into feeling productive.
Q15: I postponed discussion posts constantly because they felt low-stakes compared to assignments. I'd also avoid starting big assignments until the last minute because the anxiety of beginning something overwhelming was worse than the anxiety of the deadline approaching. It's like I needed the pressure of the deadline to override my avoidance instincts. I knew it wasn't healthy but it's how I functioned.
Q16: Deadlines basically controlled my entire relationship with the LMS. I'd be absent for days or weeks, then suddenly log in 40+ times in a short period when something was due. My engagement was completely deadline-driven—I'd complete 99% of tasks but only because I'd hyperfocus right before they were due. Without deadlines, I probably wouldn't have engaged at all.
Q17: Feedback motivated me when it was positive—seeing decent scores on assignments made me feel like the struggle was worth it. When I got my exam score back and it was 85, that gave me a boost to keep going. But waiting for feedback was torture; I'd obsessively check for grades because the uncertainty fed my anxiety. Quick feedback would have helped me a lot more.
Q18: The structure actually worked okay for me because it was clear what was required and when. Having 8 online courses meant flexibility, which I desperately needed given my unpredictable life. But the structure also assumed consistent engagement, which I couldn't provide. The mindfulness intervention they offered was surprisingly helpful for managing my anxiety spikes during high-pressure periods.
Q19: The clear deadlines and task checklists made engagement easier because I always knew what needed to happen next. What made it harder was when materials assumed you had time to explore or discuss—I didn't have that luxury. Also, some resources required consistent internet access, which was sometimes a problem for me. More downloadable content would have helped students in my situation.
Q20: I think those students might be anxious like me, constantly checking and rechecking things without actually producing work. Or they could be confused and searching for help without finding what they need. High activity doesn't mean productive activity—sometimes it's just panic clicking. They might also be dealing with personal stuff that prevents them from actually completing things despite logging in.
Q21: That definitely reflects my experience to some extent. I performed well on exams and assignments despite barely attending class and having irregular LMS activity. The key is that I was learning through other means—listening to audio, studying offline, processing information in ways the system couldn't track. Activity logs don't capture what happens in your head or outside the platform.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you log in and how often, what you click on, and whether you complete tasks. But they miss the quality of engagement—whether you're actually absorbing material or just going through motions. They can't capture offline studying, emotional state, or the life circumstances affecting your participation. They see behavior, not understanding or struggle.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about learning the material and putting in effort to understand it, even when it's hard. For me, it's not about how many times I log in but whether I'm genuinely trying to grasp concepts. Sometimes my most engaged moments happened offline, listening to lectures on repeat until something clicked. Engagement is mental investment, not just platform activity.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much anxiety I carried throughout this course, or how hard I worked to manage it through the mindfulness techniques they taught us. It wouldn't capture my offline studying, my financial stress, or the fact that my high assignment completion rate represents genuine effort despite everything working against me. It sees a struggling student with low attendance, but it doesn't see someone who fought hard to earn that Merit grade.
